title: tf Vectors and Operations
text: First extract tf vector tidyfun::dti_df dataset containing fractional anisotropy tract profiles corpus callosum (cca). printed, tf vectors show first arg value pairs subject. also extract simple 5-element vector functions regular grid: illustration, plot vector cca_five .

title: tf Vectors and Operations
text: tfd objects contain ‚Äúraw‚ÄĚ functional data: represented list evaluations \(f_i(t)|_{t=t'}\) corresponding argument vector(s) \(t'\) domain: range valid args. tfd-vector contains evaluator function defines inter-/extrapolate evaluations args tfd two subclasses: one regular data common grid one irregular sparse data. cca data irregular (values missing subjects arguments) example clearly illustrates support sparse irregular data using CD4 cell counts longitudinal study.

title: tf Vectors and Operations
text: Functional data basis representation: represented list coefficients common basis_matrix basis function evaluations vector arg-values. contains basis function defines evaluate basis functions new args differentiate integrate . tfb_spline: uses mgcv-spline bases tfb_fpc: uses functional principal components significant memory time savings:
code: refund::DTI$cca |>   object.size() |>   print(units = "Kb") ## 307.7 Kb cca |>   object.size() |>   print(units = "Kb") ## 782.4 Kb cca |>   tfb(verbose = FALSE) |>   object.size() |>   print(units = "Kb") ## 181.6 Kb

title: tf Vectors and Operations
text: default tfb() accepts arguments mgcv‚Äôs s()-syntax: basis type bs, basis dimension k, penalty order m, etc‚Ä¶ exponential families also: \(t\)-distribution, ZI-Poisson, Beta, ‚Ä¶

Max. ## 95.60 96.40 96.90 97.12 98.00 98.70 cca_five_b[1:2] ## tfb[2]: [0,1] -> [0.41581,0.6516056] in basis representation: ##  using  s(arg, bs = "cr", k = 25, sp = -1)   ## A: ‚ĖÜ‚Ėą‚Ėą‚ĖÜ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖĄ‚ĖĄ‚ĖĄ‚ĖĄ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÖ‚ĖĄ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÉ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚Ėā‚ĖĄ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÜ‚Ėá‚Ėá ## B: ‚ĖÖ‚Ėá‚ĖÜ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖĄ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚Ėā‚Ėā‚Ėā‚Ėā‚ĖÉ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÜ‚Ėá‚Ėá‚ĖÜ‚Ėá cca_five[1:2] |> tfb(bs = "tp", k = 55) ## Percentage of input data variability preserved in basis representation ## (per functional observation, approximate): ## Min. 1st Qu. title: tf Vectors and Operations
text: Function-specific (default), none, prespecified (sp), global:  Right plot shows smoothing function-specific penalization red, without penalization blue, manually set strong smoothing (sp \(\0\)) orange. ‚ÄúGlobal‚ÄĚ smoothing: estimate smoothing parameters subsample (~10%) curves apply geometric mean estimated smoothing parameters smooth curves Advantages: (much) faster optimizing penalization curve scale well largish datasets Disadvantages real borrowing information across curves (sparse functional fragment data, e.g.) still requires observations basis functions per curve subsample miss small subgroups different roughness, -/undersmooth parts data, see . Dataset heterogeneous roughness:
code: layout(t(1:2)) cca_five |> plot() cca_five_b |> plot(col = "red") cca_five |>   tfb(k = 35, penalized = FALSE) |>   lines(col = "blue") ## Percentage of input data variability preserved in basis representation ## (per functional observation, approximate): ## Min. 1st Qu.  Median Mean 3rd Qu.  Max. ## 98.5 98.6 98.7 99.0 99.6 99.6 cca_five |>   tfb(sp = 0.001) |>   lines(col = "orange") ## Percentage of input data variability preserved in basis representation ## (per functional observation, approximate): ## Min. 1st Qu.  Median Mean 3rd Qu.  Max. ## 72.60 75.90 76.50 76.54 77.20 80.50 layout(t(1:3)) clrs <- scales::alpha(sample(viridis(15)), 0.5) plot(raw, main = "raw", col = clrs) plot(tfb(raw, k = 55), main = "separate", col = clrs) ## Percentage of input data variability preserved in basis representation ## (per functional observation, approximate): ## Min. 1st Qu.  Median Mean 3rd Qu.  Max. ## 72.20 88.65 94.80 92.06 96.55 97.70 plot(tfb(raw, k = 55, global = TRUE), main = "global", col = clrs) ## Using global smoothing parameter `sp = 0` estimated on subsample of curves. ## Percentage of input data variability preserved in basis representation ## (per functional observation, approximate): ## Min. 1st Qu. title: tf Vectors and Operations
text: uses first eigenfunctions computed simple unregularized (weighted) SVD data matrix default corresponding FPC basis mean function saved tfd-object observed functions linear combinations . amount ‚Äúsmoothing‚ÄĚ can controlled (roughly!) setting minimal percentage variance explained pve  tfb_fpc currently implemented data identical (possibly non-equidistant) grids. {refunder} rfr_fpca-functions provide FPCA methods appropriate highly irregular sparse data regularized/smoothed FPCA.

title: Converting to & from `tf`
text: fda package represents functional data fd objects (basis function coefficients + basis definition). tf can convert directly using tfb_spline, re-expresses fd basis tf‚Äôs spline framework. also works fdSmooth objects returned fda::smooth.basis. resulting tfb object can used tidyfun tools:
code: # create an fd object from the Canadian weather data weather_basis <- fda::create.fourier.basis(c(0, 365), nbasis = 65) weather_fd <- fda::smooth.basis(   argvals = 1:365,   y = fda::CanadianWeather$dailyAv[, , 1],   fdParobj = weather_basis )  # convert fdSmooth to tfb weather_tf <- tfb_spline(weather_fd) ## Percentage of input data variability preserved in basis representation ## (per functional observation, approximate): ## Min. 1st Qu.  Median Mean 3rd Qu.  Max. ## 100 100 100 100 100 100 weather_tf[1:3] ## tfb[3]: [0,365] -> [-7.867481,19.46969] in basis representation: ##  using  s(arg, bs = "fourier", k = 65, sp = NA, xt = list(period = 365))   ## St. Johns: ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚Ėā‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÜ‚Ėá‚Ėá‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėá‚Ėá‚ĖÜ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÖ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚Ėā‚Ėā ## Halifax  : ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚Ėā‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÜ‚Ėá‚Ėá‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėá‚Ėá‚ĖÜ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚Ėā‚ĖĀ ## Sydney   : ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚ĖĀ‚Ėā‚ĖÉ‚ĖÉ‚ĖĄ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÖ‚ĖÜ‚Ėá‚Ėá‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėą‚Ėá‚ĖÜ‚ĖÜ‚ĖÖ‚ĖĄ‚ĖĄ‚ĖÉ‚Ėā‚Ėā tibble(   place = fda::CanadianWeather$place,   region = fda::CanadianWeather$region,   temp = weather_tf ) |>   tf_ggplot(aes(tf = temp, color = region)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.5) ## Warning: Large data expansion: 35 rows √ó 365 grid points = 12775 rows ## ‚ĄĻ This may impact memory usage and plotting performance ## ‚ĄĻ Use `arg` in `tf_ggplot()` to specify a coarser evaluation grid

title: Data Wrangling
text: Dataframes using tidyfun store functional observations can manipulated using tools dplyr, including select filter:  Operations using group_by summarize also work ‚Äď let‚Äôs look daily averages activity profiles:  One can mutate functional observations ‚Äď exponentiate log activity counts obtain original recordings:  Functions data manipulation tidyr also supported. illustrate using pivot_wider create new tfd-columns containing activity profiles day week: (Note made data less ‚Äútidy‚ÄĚ therefore generally recommended, may useful cases). ‚Äôs also possible join datasets based non-functional keys. illustrate, ‚Äôll first create pair datasets: can joined using id variable key (tidied using pivot_longer): Similar tidying can done DTI data ‚Äď let‚Äôs look average RCST tract values gender case status:
code: chf_df |>   select(id, day, activity) |>   filter(day == "Mon") |>   tf_ggplot(aes(tf = activity)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.05) ## Warning: Large data expansion: 47 rows √ó 1440 grid points = 67680 rows ## ‚ĄĻ This may impact memory usage and plotting performance ## ‚ĄĻ Use `arg` in `tf_ggplot()` to specify a coarser evaluation grid chf_df |>   group_by(day) |>   summarize(mean_act = mean(activity)) |>   tf_ggplot(aes(tf = mean_act, color = day)) +   geom_line() ## title: Curve Registration: Practical Guide and Pitfalls
text: Registration aligns functions time reducing phase variation (timing differences - ‚Äúhorizontal‚ÄĚ variability) preserving amplitude variation (shape/height differences - ‚Äúvertical‚ÄĚ variability). Use registration : analysis care timing differences want analyze timing differences separately shape differences. can interpret target template resulting warps domain terms. use registration (default) preprocessing step : Curves come clearly different shape classes/clusters (see ). Timing differences meaningful question /viewed isolation shape differences. time deformation registration creates implausible subject-specific timelines. Core problem: Registration models observed curves \(x_i(t)\) time-deformed versions template \(m(s)\), time deformation given monotone warping function \(h_i(s)\): \[ x_i(t) \approx m(h_i^{-1}(t)) \] Key interpretation: \(h_i\) maps aligned/system time observed time. \(h_i^{-1}\) maps observed time aligned time. Slope \(h_i^{-1}\) greater 1 indicates local compression observed time (events happen faster), slope less 1 indicates local dilation (events happen slower). Core workflow tf:
code: # One-shot registration (returns tf_registration object): reg <- tf_register(x, method = "...") tf_aligned(reg)   # registered/aligned curves tf_inv_warps(reg)     # estimated inverse warping functions (observed ‚Üí aligned time) tf_template(reg)  # template used summary(reg)      # alignment diagnostics plot(reg)          # 3-panel diagnostic plot  # Or step by step: warps <- tf_estimate_warps(x, method = "...") x_registered <- tf_align(x, warps)

text: Issue: Registration quality may affected strongly interpolation grid choices. Remedies: Re-evaluate fine, common grid; run sensitivity checks grid density. srvf cc methods require regular grids (tfd_reg); interpolate sparse irregular data common regular grid first. affine landmark methods also accept irregular grids (tfd_irreg). title: Curve Registration: Practical Guide and Pitfalls
text: Problem find optimal deformation function‚Äôs domain: \[ \text{Find } h_i(s) \text{ }  d\left(x_i(h_i(s)), m(s)\right) \\min \] \(x_i(t)\): observed curve subject \(\) observed times \(t\). \(m(s)\): ‚Äútemplate‚ÄĚ function register . \(h_i(s)\): warping function mapping aligned time \(s\) (‚Äúsystem time‚ÄĚ) observed time \(t\) (‚Äúwallclock time‚ÄĚ) \(\)-th curve. returned tf_estimate_warps() (called internally tf_register()), registration methods differ representations constraints (see ). \(h_i^{-1}(t)\): inverse warping (observed time \(\\) system time) used tf_align() align observed curves, can inspected/extracted via tf_inv_warps(<tf_registration>). \(d(\cdot, \cdot)\): distance dissimilarity minimize (e.g., \(L_2\) distance derivatives \(\int (x'_i(h_i(s))h'_i(s) - m'(s))^2 ds)\)). Key references: Marron et al.¬†(2015) provide overview amplitude phase variation FDA. Ramsay & Silverman (2005, Functional Data Analysis, Ch. 7‚Äď8) cover landmark continuous registration. Srivastava & Klassen (2016, Functional Shape Data Analysis) develop elastic/SRVF framework depth.

title: Curve Registration: Practical Guide and Pitfalls
text: Represents curve via square root velocity function (SRVF), \(q(t) = \dot{x}(t) / \sqrt{|\dot{x}(t)|}\), aligns curves minimizing \(L_2\) distances (aligned) SRVFs. corresponds minimizing elastic distance metric functions modulo reparameterization (.e.¬†‚Äúwarping‚ÄĚ) transforms non-linear alignment problem simpler optimization Hilbert sphere3. See Srivastava et al.¬†(2011) Tucker et al.¬†(2013) fdasrvf implementation. Optimization: Dynamic programming space diffeomorphisms4 (via fdasrvf). Template: Karcher mean shape manifold (iterative; see footnote template table ), user-supplied. Domain-preserving: Yes ‚ÄĒ warps satisfy \(h(t_\min) = t_\min\) \(h(t_\max) = t_\max\). Key arguments: method = "srvf". Pass template override Karcher mean. Control warp flexibility via lambda (default 0 unrestricted/unpenalized warps) penalty_method ("roughness" (default), "geodesic", "norm"). Strengths: clean low-noise data, SRVF often achieves best warp recovery complex phase variation. Makes assumptions warping function shape (except domain preservation monotonicity). Rankings shift noise level template shape, always compare least one alternative. Weaknesses: Sensitive noise SRSFs involve numerical derivatives amplify observation noise. Pre-smoothing inputs (e.g., tfb(x, k = 25)) registration reduces warp error (measured warp MISE) 50‚Äď70% moderate noise. Also grid-sensitive: avoid grids >200 points noisy data (see Sparse irregular grids). Shows variable performance template estimation compared methods.

title: Curve Registration: Practical Guide and Pitfalls
text: Estimates smooth monotone warping functions maximizing integrated cross-correlation derivative registered curve first eigenfunction derivatives‚Äô covariance, represents dominant common shape sample (criterion crit=2, default) minimizing squared differences aligned function template (criterion crit=1, recommended). current tf, implemented tf-native dense-grid optimizer monotone spline warps rather older fda::register.fd() backend. See Ramsay & Li (1998, JRSS-B) Ramsay & Silverman (2005, Functional Data Analysis, Ch. 8). Optimization: tf-native dense-grid optimization continuous registration criterion monotone spline warps. Template: Arithmetic mean, re-estimated max_iter iterations (default 3), user-supplied. Domain-preserving: Yes. Key arguments: method = "cc". Use crit = 2 (default) maximize proportion variance explained first eigenfunction registered sample, crit = 1 minimize integrated squared differences. Control warp flexibility via B-spline basis dimension warping functions nbasis (default 6), wiggliness via penalty parameter lambda (default 0 penalization), optimizer tolerances via conv iterlim. experience, crit = 1 without penalization tends considerably less reliable crit = 2 penalized variants ‚ÄĒ unpenalized L2 criterion can produce strongly distorted warps. Strengths: noise-robust grid-insensitive method benchmarks. Tends stable method template estimation. Benefits penalization (lambda > 0) conditions. Weaknesses: Results sensitive criterion choice spline basis configuration.

title: Curve Registration: Practical Guide and Pitfalls
text: Models warps linear functions \(h(s) = \cdot s + b\), three sub-types: type = "shift" (default): \(= 1\), horizontal translation (\(b\) free). type = "scale": uniform time scaling (\(\) free, \(b\) derived centering). type = "shift_scale": parameters free. curve aligned independently via bounded L-BFGS-B optimization L2 distance template. See Ramsay & Silverman (2005, Ch. 7) Wang & Gasser (1997) context shift/scale alignment models. Template: Arithmetic mean, user-supplied. Domain-preserving: ‚ÄĒ warps can shift scale outside observed domain, producing boundary NAs tf_align(). Input grid: Supports regular irregular grids (tfd_reg tfd_irreg). Key arguments: method = "affine", type, shift_range scale_range set upper lower limits time shifts \(b_i\) time scales \(a_i\). Strengths: fast (typically order magnitude faster SRVF), highly interpretable parameters. Weaknesses: capture non-linear timing deformation. Results often shortened functions substantial leading /trailing NAs (strong shifts/scales).

title: Curve Registration: Practical Guide and Pitfalls
text: Constructs piecewise linear warps mapping user-specified landmark positions target positions. continuous optimization performed ‚ÄĒ warp fully determined landmark correspondence. See Kneip & Gasser (1992, Annals Statistics) Ramsay & Silverman (2005, Ch. 7). Template: Defined column-wise means landmark time points matrix, user-supplied template_landmarks. Domain-preserving: Yes ‚ÄĒ domain endpoints used boundary anchors. Input grid: Supports regular irregular grids (tfd_reg tfd_irreg). Key arguments: method = "landmark", landmarks (required: \(n \times k\) matrix landmark positions), template_landmarks (optional target positions). Strengths: Fast deterministic. benchmarks, landmark registration robust outlier contamination: warp MISE degraded ~1.6√ó 30% contamination, compared 2.6‚Äď3.7√ó iterative methods. Weaknesses: Requires ability identify reliable, consistent landmarks across curves. Fragile features ambiguous often simplistic curves identifiable features (e.g., one peak per curve). Landmark detection: tf_landmarks_extrema() can automatically detect local maxima, minima, zero crossings cluster across curves - usually better pre-smooth noisy inputs tf_smooth() tfb calling function. Results typically better user-defined landmarks based domain knowledge.

title: Curve Registration: Practical Guide and Pitfalls
text: Berkeley growth data contains height measurements 39 boys 54 girls aged 1‚Äď18. Growth velocity curves (first derivatives height) show prominent pubertal growth spurt whose timing varies substantially individuals ‚ÄĒ natural target registration. use subset girls5 dataset illustrate data representation, penalization, landmark choice affect registration quality.
code: growth <- tf::growth |> dplyr::filter(gender == "female")  # Raw velocity via finite differences ‚ÄĒ noisy, only 30 midpoints from 31 measurements: growth$raw_vel <- tf_derive(growth$height)  # Smooth velocity via spline representation on a much denser grid, then derive analytically: growth$smooth_vel <- tfb(   growth$height,   k = 15,   bs = "tp",   arg = seq(1, 18, l = 80),   global = TRUE   # family = gaussian(link = "log") # ensures positivity of velocity ) |>   tf_derive() #> Using global smoothing parameter `sp = 0.008` estimated on subsample of curves. #> Percentage of input data variability preserved in basis representation #> (per functional observation, approximate): #> Min. 1st Qu.  Median Mean 3rd Qu.  Max. #> 100 100 100 100 100 100

text: raw finite-difference velocity estimates jagged (30 irregularly spaced points original measurement grid). SRVF registration noisy inputs tends produce wiggly warps chase noise rather genuine phase variation. Converting smooth representation first gives SRVF cleaner input much better results.  top row shows extreme, biologically implausible warps (.e.¬†mapping chronological ages 5-8 ‚Äúregistered‚ÄĚ ages < 2) local wiggles likely reflect noise raw velocity estimates rather genuine timing differences. bottom row, using smoother velocity curves, produces simpler less extreme warps capture pubertal timing variation ‚ÄĒ main feature interest.
code: # SRVF on raw (noisy) velocity: reg_raw_obj <- tf_register(growth$raw_vel, method = "srvf") inv_warp_raw <- tf_inv_warps(reg_raw_obj) reg_raw <- tf_aligned(reg_raw_obj)  # SRVF on smooth velocity: reg_smooth_obj <- tf_register(growth$smooth_vel, method = "srvf") inv_warp_smooth <- tf_inv_warps(reg_smooth_obj) reg_smooth <- tf_aligned(reg_smooth_obj) reg_brks <- range(c(tf_evaluations(reg_raw), tf_evaluations(reg_smooth))) |>   (\(x) seq(x[1], x[2], l = 13))()  layout(t(matrix(1:8, 4, 2)))  plot(growth$raw_vel, main = "Raw Velocity (finite diff.)",      xlab = "Age [years]", ylab = "cm/year", lwd = 0.8, ylim = c(0, 30)) plot(inv_warp_raw, points = FALSE, main = "SRVF Inverse Warps (raw)",      xlab = "Chronological Age", ylab = "Registered Age", lwd = 0.8) abline(0, 1, lty = 3) plot(reg_raw, main = "SRVF Registered (raw)",      xlab = "Registered Age [years]", ylab = "cm/year", lwd = 0.8, ylim = c(0, 30)) plot(reg_raw, main = "SRVF Registered (raw)",      type = "lasagna", col = hcl.colors(12, rev = TRUE), breaks = reg_brks)  plot(growth$smooth_vel, main = "Smooth Velocity (tfb deriv.)",      xlab = "Age [years]", ylab = "cm/year", lwd = 0.8, ylim = c(0, 30)) plot(inv_warp_smooth, points = FALSE, main = "SRVF Inverse Warps (smooth)",      xlab = "Chronological Age", ylab = "Registered Age", lwd = 0.8) abline(0, 1, lty = 3) plot(reg_smooth, main = "SRVF Registered (smooth)",      xlab = "Registered Age [years]", ylab = "cm/year", lwd = 0.8, ylim = c(0, 30)) plot(reg_smooth, main = "SRVF Registered (smooth)",      type = "lasagna", col = hcl.colors(12, rev = TRUE), breaks = reg_brks)

text: Landmark registration avoids continuous optimization entirely ‚ÄĒ constructs piecewise linear warps (user-specified) correspondence curve features. growth velocity, natural landmarks end rapid infant growth (velocity drops lot initial velocity), pre-pubertal trough (minimum velocity puberty growth spurt), pubertal peak (maximum velocity puberty). identify searching minimum age 5‚Äď(peak-1) maximum age 8‚Äď17 smooth velocity curve.  piecewise linear warps align pubertal peaks well, pubertal trough infant growth landmarks anchoring earlier phases. Compared SRVF, warps simpler fully interpretable ‚ÄĒ segment directly corresponds physiological period (early childhood, late childhood, pubertal acceleration, post-peak deceleration). trade-landmark registration adapt landmarks (alignment successive landmarks depends entirely two anchor points) landmark definition often somewhat arbitrary (c.f. definition ‚Äúend rapid infant growth‚ÄĚ ‚Ä¶).
code: # End of rapid infant growth:  #    less than 2/3 of max early childhood growth (1-3) velocity before age 5 growth_slows <- growth$smooth_vel |> tf_zoom(begin = 1, end = 5) |>    tf_where(value < 0.66 * max(value[1:10]), return = "first") |> unlist()  # Pubertal peak: maximum velocity in age 8--17 growth_peaks <- growth$smooth_vel |> tf_zoom(begin = 8, end = 17) |>    tf_where(value == max(value)) |> unlist()  # Pre-pubertal trough: minimum velocity between age 5 and 1 year before the peak growth_troughs <- growth$smooth_vel |> tf_zoom(begin = 5, end = growth_peaks - 1) |>    tf_where(value == min(value)) |> unlist()  #> ‚úĖ `tf_zoom()` was called with varying start or end points -- converting to #>   <tfd>.  # Build landmark matrix and register: growth_lm <- cbind(slowdown = growth_slows, trough = growth_troughs, peak = growth_peaks) reg_lm_obj <- tf_register(growth$smooth_vel, method = "landmark", landmarks = growth_lm) inv_warp_lm <- tf_inv_warps(reg_lm_obj) reg_lm <- tf_aligned(reg_lm_obj) if (!exists("reg_brks")) {   reg_brks <- range(c(tf_evaluations(growth$smooth_vel), tf_evaluations(reg_lm))) |>     (\(x) seq(x[1], x[2], l = 13))() } layout(t(1:4)) plot(growth$smooth_vel, main = "Smooth Velocity",      xlab = "Age [years]", ylab = "cm/year", lwd = 0.8) plot(inv_warp_lm, points = FALSE, main = "Landmark Inverse Warps",      xlab = "Chronological Age", ylab = "Registered Age", lwd = 0.8) abline(0, 1, lty = 3) plot(reg_lm, main = "Landmark Registered",      xlab = "Registered Age [years]", ylab = "cm/year", lwd = 0.8) plot(reg_lm, main = "Landmark Registered",      type = "lasagna", col = hcl.colors(12, rev = TRUE), breaks = reg_brks)

text: goal tidyfun provide accessible well-documented software makes functional data analysis R easy ‚Äď specifically data wrangling exploratory analysis. achieved introducing new data type (tf). title: Create a tf-aware ggplot ‚ÄĒ tf_ggplot
text: tf_ggplot supports following tf-specific aesthetics: tf: Maps tf object y aesthetic (shorthand tf_y) tf_x: Maps tf object x aesthetic tf_y: Maps tf object y aesthetic tf_ymin: Maps tf object ymin aesthetic (ribbons) tf_ymax: Maps tf object ymax aesthetic (ribbons) tf aesthetics used, data automatically transformed: tf objects evaluated common grid function becomes multiple rows (one per evaluation point) Group identifiers created maintain function identity Non-tf columns replicated appropriately

title: Create a tf-aware ggplot ‚ÄĒ tf_ggplot
code: # Basic usage data <- data.frame( id = 1:10, group = sample(c("A", "B"), 10, replace = TRUE) ) data$f <- tf_rgp(10)  # Method 1: tf aesthetic in constructor tf_ggplot(data, ggplot2::aes(tf = f, color = group)) + ggplot2::geom_line()   # Method 2: tf aesthetic in geom (equivalent) tf_ggplot(data) + ggplot2::geom_line(ggplot2::aes(tf = f, color = group))   # Confidence bands tf_ggplot(data) +   ggplot2::geom_ribbon(     ggplot2::aes(tf_ymin = mean(f) - sd(f), tf_ymax = mean(f) + sd(f)),     alpha = 0.3   ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(ggplot2::aes(tf = mean(f)))

what: tidyfun: Tidy Functional Data Wrangling and Visualization ‚ÄĒ tidyfun-package
title: tidyfun: Tidy Functional Data Wrangling and Visualization ‚ÄĒ tidyfun-package
text: tidyfun makes data wrangling exploratory analysis functional data R easier.tidyfun based classes methods defined package tf provides: new data types representing functional data: tf::tfd() & tf::tfb() arithmetic & comparison operators (tf::tfgroupgenerics()), descriptive statistics: e.g. tf::mean.tf(), tf::median.tf() graphics functions data: tf::plot.tf(), autoplot.tf(), geom_spaghetti(), gglasagna() functions smoothing (tf::tf_smooth.tfd()), differentiation integration (tf::tf_derive.tfd()) tidyverse-verbs handling functional data inside data frames: tf_gather() etc.  Also see vignette("Introducing tidyfun", "tidyfun") brief introduction.

title: tidyfun 0.0.995
text: Initial CRAN submission. Functional data types (tfd, tfb) data frame columns via tf package. tidyverse-compatible data wrangling: tf_gather, tf_spread, tf_nest, tf_unnest. ggplot2 geoms functional data: geom_spaghetti, geom_meatballs, geom_capellini, geom_errorband, gglasagna. tf_ggplot() tf-aware ggplot construction standard geoms. Functional boxplots via geom_funcboxplot.
